{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Shrine'''|熊野神社|''Kumano Jinja''}} is a [[shinto shrine]]. There is a many Kumano Shrines in Japan. Famous Kumano Shrines are as follows. *[[Kumano Sanzan]] -- Sōhonsha (the head shrine) of Kumano Shrines. **[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] **[[Kumano Hongu Taisha]] **[[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] *[[Kumanotaisha Shrine (Yamagata)]] (Nanyo, Yamagata) *[[Kumanotaisha Shrine (Shimane)]] (Matsue, Shimane) *[[Kumano Kōdai Shrine]] (Karuizawa, Nagano)

They include: *[[Kumano Sanzan]] 熊野三山 -- Sōhonsha (the head shrine) of Kumano Shrines. **[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] 熊野速玉大社, [[Wakayama]] Prefecture, Shingu city **[[Kumano Hongu Taisha]] 熊野本宮大社, [[Wakayama]] Prefecture, Tanabe city **[[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] 熊野那智大社, [[Wakayama]] Prefecture, Nachikatsuura town *[[Kumanotaisha Shrine (Yamagata)]] (Nanyo, Yamagata) *[[Kumanotaisha Shrine (Shimane)]] (Matsue, Shimane) *[[Kumano Kōdai Shrine]] (Karuizawa, Nagano)

revision.text:
'''{{nihongo|Kumano Shrine|熊野神社}}''' is a type of [[shinto shrine]]. There are many Kumano shrines in Japan. They include: *[[Kumano Sanzan]] — Sōhonsha (the head shrine) of Kumano Shrines. **[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]<!-- 熊野速玉大社-->, [[Wakayama]] Prefecture, Shingu city **[[Kumano Hongu Taisha]], [[Wakayama]] Prefecture, Tanabe city **[[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], [[Wakayama]] Prefecture, Nachikatsuura town *[[Kumanotaisha Shrine (Yamagata)]] (Nanyo, Yamagata) *[[Kumanotaisha Shrine (Shimane)]] (Matsue, Shimane) *[[Kumano Kōdai Shrine]] (Karuizawa, Nagano)
== Bibliography == * D. Max Moerman, ''Localizing Paradise: Kumano Pilgrimage and the Religious Landscape of Premodern Japan''. Harvard University Press, 2004. ISBN 0674013956
{{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]]
[[it:Tempio di Kumano]] [[ja:熊野神社]]

'''{{nihongo|Kumano Shrine|熊野神社}}''' is [[shinto shrine]] which enshrines the {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} ''kami''. There are more than 3000 Kumano shrines in Japan, and each has received its kami from the head shrine through a process of subdivision called {{nihongo|[[kanjō]]|勧請}}. They include: *[[Kumano Sanzan]] — Sōhonsha (the head shrine) of Kumano Shrines. **[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]<!-- 熊野速玉大社-->, [[Wakayama]] Prefecture, Shingu city **[[Kumano Hongu Taisha]], [[Wakayama]] Prefecture, Tanabe city **[[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], [[Wakayama]] Prefecture, Nachikatsuura town *[[Kumanotaisha Shrine (Yamagata)]] (Nanyo, Yamagata) *[[Kumanotaisha Shrine (Shimane)]] (Matsue, Shimane) *[[Kumano Kōdai Shrine]] (Karuizawa, Nagano)
== Bibliography == * D. Max Moerman, ''Localizing Paradise: Kumano Pilgrimage and the Religious Landscape of Premodern Japan''. Harvard University Press, 2004. ISBN 0674013956 * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan"] accessed on June 12, 2008 * Japanese Wikipedia article "熊野神社" accessed on June 12, 2008 {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]]
[[it:Tempio di Kumano]] [[ja:熊野神社]]

[[Image:Kumano_Jinja_12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano Jinja in [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama prefecture]]]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano Jinja|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] which enshrines the {{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}} ''kami''. There are more than 3000 Kumano shrines in Japan, and each has received its ''kami'' from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}. They include:
*[[Kumano Sanzan]] — Sōhonsha ("head shrine") of the Kumano Shrines. **{{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}}, [[Wakayama]] Prefecture, [[Shingū, Wakayama|Shingu]] **[[Kumano Hongu Taisha]], [[Wakayama]] Prefecture, [[Tanabe, Wakayama|Tanabe]] **[[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], [[Wakayama]] Prefecture, [[Nachikatsuura, Wakayama|Nachikatsuura]] *[[Kumanotaisha Shrine (Yamagata)]] (Nanyo, Yamagata) *[[Kumanotaisha Shrine (Shimane)]] (Matsue, Shimane) *[[Kumano Kōdai Shrine]] (Karuizawa, Nagano)
== Bibliography == * D. Max Moerman, ''Localizing Paradise: Kumano Pilgrimage and the Religious Landscape of Premodern Japan''. Harvard University Press, 2004. There are more than 3000 Kumano shrines in Japan, and each has received its ''kami'' from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}. They include:
== The Kumano Sanzan == The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex, which includes the {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ([[Wakayama]] Prefecture, [[Shingū, Wakayama|Shingu]]), the [[Kumano Hongu Taisha]] ([[Wakayama]] Prefecture, [[Tanabe, Wakayama|Tanabe]]), and the [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ([[Wakayama]] Prefecture, [[Nachikatsuura, Wakayama|Nachikatsuura]])<ref name="ss">Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan"</ref>. The three are the Sōhonsha ("head shrines") of all Kumano Shrines and lie at between 20 to 40 km of distance one from the other<ref name="ss"/>. They are connected by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|"Kumano Sankeimichi"|熊野参詣道}}<ref name="ss"/>.
Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship, however, under the influence of Buddhism (see the article [[Shinbutsu shūgō]]), they came to be worshiped as the three deities of Kumano<ref name="ss"/>. Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of Indian [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (the so-called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with Buddist gods [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]] and Senju [[Kannon]]<ref name="ss"/>. Thereafter the Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination<ref name="ss"/>.

There are more than 3000 Kumano shrines in Japan, and each has received its ''kami'' from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}. They include:
== The Kumano Sanzan == The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex, which includes the {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ([[Wakayama]] Prefecture, [[Shingū, Wakayama|Shingu]]), the [[Kumano Hongu Taisha]] ([[Wakayama]] Prefecture, [[Tanabe, Wakayama|Tanabe]]), and the [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ([[Wakayama]] Prefecture, [[Nachikatsuura, Wakayama|Nachikatsuura]])<ref name="ss">Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan"</ref>. The three are the Sōhonsha ("head shrines") of all Kumano Shrines and lie at between 20 to 40 km of distance one from the other<ref name="ss"/>. They are connected by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|"Kumano Sankeimichi"|熊野参詣道}}<ref name="ss"/>.
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times, and therefore predates all modern religions<ref name="ks">Kumano Sanzan</ref>. The area was and still is considered a place favorable to healing<ref name="ks"/>. Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship but in the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, they came to be worshiped as the three deities of Kumano<ref name="ks"/>. Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of Indian [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (the so-called ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three then came to be associated with Buddhist gods [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]] and Senju [[Kannon]]<ref name="ss"/>. The site became therefore a unique example of fusion of Buddhism and Japanese original cults (see the article [[Shinbutsu shūgō]])<ref name="ks"/>. Thereafter the Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination<ref name="ss"/>. In they 11th century pilgrims were mostly members of the [[Emperor_of_Japan|Imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners<ref name="ks"/>. The Kumano Sanzan also includes two Buddhist temples, Seiganto-ji and Fudarakusan-ji<ref name="ks"/>.

Thereafter the Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination<ref name="ss"/>. In they 11th century pilgrims were mostly members of the [[Emperor_of_Japan|Imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners<ref name="ks"/>.

[[it:Tempio di Kumano]] [[ja:熊野神社]]

[[Image:Kumano_Jinja_12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama prefecture]]]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a [[Shinto shrine]] which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi ({{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}})<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>. There are more than 3000 Kumano shrines in Japan, and each has received its ''kami'' from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}. The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex, which includes {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ([[Wakayama]] Prefecture, [[Shingū, Wakayama|Shingu]]), [[Kumano Hongu Taisha]] ([[Wakayama]] Prefecture, [[Tanabe, Wakayama|Tanabe]]), and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ([[Wakayama]] Prefecture, [[Nachikatsuura, Wakayama|Nachikatsuura]])<ref name="ss">Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan"</ref>.
== The Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seigantō-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie at between 20 to 40 km of distance one from the other<ref name="ss"/>. They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|"Kumano Sankeimichi"|熊野参詣道}}<ref name="ss"/>. The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, Seigantō-ji and Fudarakusan-ji<ref name="ks"/>.
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times, and therefore predates all modern religions<ref name="ks">Kumano Sanzan</ref>. The area was, and still, is considered a place of physical healing<ref name="ks"/>. Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship but in the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano<ref name="ks"/>. Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of Indian [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with Buddhist gods [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]] and Senju [[Kannon]]<ref name="ss"/>. The site became therefore a unique example of fusion of Buddhism and Japanese original cults (see the article [[Shinbutsu shūgō]])<ref name="ks"/>. Thereafter the Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination<ref name="ss"/>. In they 11th century pilgrims were mostly members of the [[Emperor_of_Japan|Imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners<ref name="ks"/>.
== Other important Kumano shrines == *[[Kumanotaisha Shrine (Yamagata)]] (Nanyo, Yamagata) *[[Kumanotaisha Shrine (Shimane)]] (Matsue, Shimane) *[[Kumano Kōdai Shrine]] (Karuizawa, Nagano)

[[Image:Kumano_Jinja_12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama prefecture]]]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi ({{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}})<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>. There are more than 3000 Kumano shrines in Japan, and each has received its ''kami'' from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}. The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex, which includes {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ([[Wakayama]] Prefecture, [[Shingū, Wakayama|Shingu]]), [[Kumano Hongu Taisha]] ([[Wakayama]] Prefecture, [[Tanabe, Wakayama|Tanabe]]), and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ([[Wakayama]] Prefecture, [[Nachikatsuura, Wakayama|Nachikatsuura]])<ref name="ss">Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan"</ref>.
==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 to 40 km from each other<ref name="ss"/>. They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|"Kumano Sankeimichi"|熊野参詣道}}<ref name="ss"/>. The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]]<ref name="ks"/>.
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan<ref name="ks">Kumano Sanzan</ref>. The area was, and still is, considered a place of physical healing<ref name="ks"/>. Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship, but in the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano<ref name="ks"/>. Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of Indian [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with the Buddhas [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]] and Senju [[Kannon]]<ref name="ss"/>. The site became, therefore, a unique example of fusion between Buddhism and Japanese original cults (see the article [[Shinbutsu shūgō]])<ref name="ks"/>. Thereafter the Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination<ref name="ss"/>. In the 11th century pilgrims were mostly members of the [[Emperor_of_Japan|Imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners<ref name="ks"/>.
== Other important Kumano shrines == *[[Kumanotaisha Shrine (Yamagata)]] (Nanyo, Yamagata) *[[Kumanotaisha Shrine (Shimane)]] (Matsue, Shimane) *[[Kumano Kōdai Shrine]] (Karuizawa, Nagano)

==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 to 40 km from each other<ref name="ss"/>. They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|"Kumano Sankeimichi"|熊野参詣道}}<ref name="ss"/>. The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]]<ref name="ks"/>.
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan<ref name="ks">Kumano Sanzan</ref>. The area was, and still is, considered a place of physical healing<ref name="ks"/>. Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship, but in the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano<ref name="ks"/>. Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of Indian [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with the Buddhas [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]] and Senju [[Kannon]]<ref name="ss"/>. The site became, therefore, a unique example of fusion between Buddhism and Japanese original cults (see the article [[Shinbutsu shūgō]])<ref name="ks"/>. Thereafter the Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination<ref name="ss"/>. In the 11th century pilgrims were mostly members of the [[Emperor_of_Japan|Imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners<ref name="ks"/>.
== Other important Kumano shrines == *[[Kumanotaisha Shrine (Yamagata)]] (Nanyo, Yamagata) *[[Kumanotaisha Shrine (Shimane)]] (Matsue, Shimane) *[[Kumano Kōdai Shrine]] (Karuizawa, Nagano)

[[Image:Kumano Jinja 12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in [[Kurashiki, Okayama]]]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi ({{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}}).<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> There are more than 3000 Kumano shrines in Japan, and each has received its [[kami]] from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}.
The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex of [[Wakayama Prefecture]], which includes {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ([[Shingū, Wakayama]]), [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] ([[Tanabe, Wakayama]]) and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ([[Nachikatsuura, Wakayama]]).<ref name="ss">Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan"</ref>
==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 to 40 km from each other.<ref name="ss"/> They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|"Kumano Sankeimichi"|熊野参詣道}}.<ref name="ss"/> The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]].<ref name="ks"/>
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan.<ref name="ks">Kumano Sanzan</ref> The area was, and still is, considered a place of physical healing.<ref name="ks"/> Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship, but in the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of Indian [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with the Buddhas Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''sahasrabhūja'' (Senju Kannon, "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokite.C5.9Bvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book | last = [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] | first = [[Mark Teeuwen]] (editors) | coauthors = | title = Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | date = July 2000 | location = Honolulu | language = | isbn = 978-0-8248-2363-4 | oclc = 43487317 }}, p. 194</ref> The site became, therefore, a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] or the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese [[ethnic religion|indigenous religion]].<ref name="ks"/> Thereafter the Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century pilgrims were mostly members of the [[Emperor of Japan|Imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> Pilgrims were called the "Kumano ants" (蟻の熊野) because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.
== Other important Kumano shrines == *[[Kumano Shrine (Yamagata)]]
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * [http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan.html Kumano Sanzan] accessed on December 1, 2008 * Japanese Wikipedia article "[[:ja:熊野神社]]" accessed on June 12, 2008 * D. Max Moerman, ''Localizing Paradise: Kumano Pilgrimage and the Religious Landscape of Premodern Japan''. Harvard University Press, 2004. ISBN 0674013956 * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan"] accessed on June 12, 2008
==External links== {{commons category|Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)}} * [http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau]
[[Category:Shinto shrines in Japan]]

[[Image:Kumano Jinja 12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in [[Kurashiki, Okayama]]]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi ({{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}}).<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> There are more than 3000 Kumano shrines in Japan, and each has received its [[kami]] from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}.
The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex of [[Wakayama Prefecture]], which includes {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ([[Shingū, Wakayama]]), [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] ([[Tanabe, Wakayama]]) and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ([[Nachikatsuura, Wakayama]]).<ref name="ss">[http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan"]</ref>
==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 to 40 km from each other.<ref name="ss"/> They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|"Kumano Sankeimichi"|熊野参詣道}}.<ref name="ss"/> The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]].<ref name="ks"/>
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan.<ref name="ks">[http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ Kumano Sanzan]</ref> The area was, and still is, considered a place of physical healing.<ref name="ks"/> Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship, but in the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of Indian [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with the Buddhas Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''sahasrabhūja'' (Senju Kannon, "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokite.C5.9Bvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book | last = [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] | first = [[Mark Teeuwen]] (editors) | coauthors = | title = Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | date = July 2000 | location = Honolulu | language = | isbn = 978-0-8248-2363-4 | oclc = 43487317 }}, p. 194</ref> The site became, therefore, a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] or the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese [[ethnic religion|indigenous religion]].<ref name="ks"/> Thereafter the Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century pilgrims were mostly members of the [[Emperor of Japan|Imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> Pilgrims were called the "Kumano ants" (蟻の熊野) because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.
== See also == *[[Kumano Shrine (Yamagata)]] *[[Tale of Heike]]<ref name=Watson>{{Cite book |translator=Burton Watson |title=The Tales of the Heike |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2006 |ISBN=9780231138031 |page=133}}</ref>

== References == * D. Max Moerman, ''Localizing Paradise: Kumano Pilgrimage and the Religious Landscape of Premodern Japan''. Harvard University Press, 2004. ISBN 0674013956 * Moerman, David (1997). [https://web.archive.org/web/20140928090538/http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2638 The ideology of landscape and the theater of state: Insei pilgrimage to Kumano (1090–1220)], Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 24 (3-4), 347-374

Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''sahasrabhūja'' (Senju Kannon, "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokite.C5.9Bvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book | last = [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] | first = [[Mark Teeuwen]] (editors) | coauthors = | title = Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | date = July 2000 | location = Honolulu | language = | isbn = 978-0-8248-2363-4 | oclc = 43487317 }}, p. 194</ref> The site became, therefore, a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] or the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese [[ethnic religion|indigenous religion]].<ref name="ks"/> Thereafter the Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century pilgrims were mostly members of the [[Emperor of Japan|Imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> The visit was referred to as the "Kumano ant pilgrimage" (蟻の熊野参り) because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.

[[Image:Kumano Jinja 12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in [[Kurashiki, Okayama]]]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi ({{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}}).<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> There are more than 3000 Kumano shrines in Japan, and each has received its [[kami]] from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}.
The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex of [[Wakayama Prefecture]], which includes {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ([[Shingū, Wakayama]]), [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] ([[Tanabe, Wakayama]]) and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ([[Nachikatsuura, Wakayama]]).<ref name="ss">[http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan"]</ref>

[[Image:Kumano Jinja 12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama]]]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū]], Shingū, and Nachi ({{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}}).<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> There are more than 3000 Kumano shrines in Japan, and each has received its [[kami]] from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}.
The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex of [[Wakayama Prefecture]], which includes {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ([[Shingū, Wakayama]]), [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] ([[Tanabe, Wakayama]]) and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ([[Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture).<ref name="ss">[http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan"]</ref>
==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 to 40 km from each other.<ref name="ss"/> They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Kodō]]|熊野古道}}.<ref name="ss"/> The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]].<ref name="ks"/>
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan.<ref name="ks">[http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ Kumano Sanzan]</ref> The area was, and still is, considered a place of physical healing.<ref name="ks"/> Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship, but in the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with the Buddhas. Kuniyasutamahime became associated with ''Sahasrabhūja'' [[Avalokiteśvara]] (''Senju Kannon'', "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokite.C5.9Bvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (''Yakushi Nyōrai'') and [[Amitābha]] (''Amida Nyōrai'').<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|authorlink1=John Breen (scholar)|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|authorlink2=Mark Teeuwen|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZmMAQAAQBAJ|year=2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|p=194}}</ref> The site became, therefore, a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]], the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese [[ethnic religion|indigenous religion]].<ref name="ks"/> Thereafter the Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century pilgrims were mostly members of the [[emperor of Japan|imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> The visit was referred to as the {{nihongo|"Kumano ant pilgrimage"|蟻の熊野参り||Ari no Kumano mairi}} because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.

==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 and 40 km from each other.<ref name="ss"/> They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Kodō]]|熊野古道}}.<ref name="ss"/> The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]].<ref name="ks"/>
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan.<ref name="ks">[http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ Kumano Sanzan]</ref> The area was, and still is, considered a place of physical healing.<ref name="ks"/> Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship, but in the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with the Buddhas. Kuniyasutamahime became associated with ''Sahasrabhūja'' [[Avalokiteśvara]] (''Senju Kannon'', "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokite.C5.9Bvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (''Yakushi Nyōrai'') and [[Amitābha]] (''Amida Nyōrai'').<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|authorlink1=John Breen (scholar)|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|authorlink2=Mark Teeuwen|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZmMAQAAQBAJ|year=2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|p=194}}</ref> The site became, therefore, a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]], the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese [[ethnic religion|indigenous religion]].<ref name="ks"/> Thereafter the Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century pilgrims were mostly members of the [[emperor of Japan|imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> The visit was referred to as the {{nihongo|"Kumano ant pilgrimage"|蟻の熊野参り||Ari no Kumano mairi}} because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.

Harvard University Press, 2004. {{ISBN|0674013956}} * Moerman, David (1997). [https://web.archive.org/web/20140928090538/http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2638 The ideology of landscape and the theater of state: Insei pilgrimage to Kumano (1090–1220)], Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 24 (3-4), 347-374

[[Image:Kumano Jinja 12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama]]]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū]], Shingū, and Nachi ({{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}}).<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> There are more than 3,000 Kumano shrines in Japan, and each has received its [[kami]] from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}.
The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], which comprises {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ([[Shingū, Wakayama]]), [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] ([[Tanabe, Wakayama]]) and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ([[Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture).<ref name="ss">{{Cite web |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |title=Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan" |access-date=2008-06-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090303221919/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |archive-date=2009-03-03 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 and 40 km from each other.<ref name="ss"/> They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Kodō]]|熊野古道}}.<ref name="ss"/> The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]].<ref name="ks"/>
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan.<ref name="ks">[http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ Kumano Sanzan]</ref> The area is still considered a place of physical healing.<ref name="ks"/> Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship, but in the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with Buddhas. Kuniyasutamahime became associated with ''Sahasrabhūja'' [[Avalokiteśvara]] (''Senju Kannon'', "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (''Yakushi Nyōrai'') and [[Amitābha]] (''Amida Nyōrai'').<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|authorlink1=John Breen (scholar)|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|authorlink2=Mark Teeuwen|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZmMAQAAQBAJ|year=2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|page=194}}</ref> The site became, therefore, a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]], the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese [[ethnic religion|indigenous religion]].<ref name="ks"/> Thereafter the Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century pilgrims were mostly members of the [[emperor of Japan|imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> The visit was referred to as the {{nihongo|"Kumano ant pilgrimage"|蟻の熊野参り||Ari no Kumano mairi}} because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.
== See also == *[[Kumano Shrine (Yamagata)]] *''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'' *''[[Acts of Worship]]''
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * D. Max Moerman, ''Localizing Paradise: Kumano Pilgrimage and the Religious Landscape of Premodern Japan''. Harvard University Press, 2004. {{ISBN|0674013956}} * Moerman, David (1997). [https://web.archive.org/web/20140928090538/http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2638 The ideology of landscape and the theater of state: Insei pilgrimage to Kumano (1090–1220)], Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 24 (3-4), 347-374
==External links== {{commonscat|Kumano shrines}} *Japanese Wikipedia article "[[:ja:熊野神社]]" accessed on June 12, 2008 *[http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau]
{{Authority control}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Kumano shrines| ]]

== See also == *[[Kumano Shrine (Yamagata)]] *''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'' *''[[Acts of Worship]]''
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * D. Max Moerman, ''Localizing Paradise: Kumano Pilgrimage and the Religious Landscape of Premodern Japan''. Harvard University Press, 2004. {{ISBN|0674013956}} * Moerman, David (1997). [https://web.archive.org/web/20140928090538/http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2638 The ideology of landscape and the theater of state: Insei pilgrimage to Kumano (1090–1220)], Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 24 (3-4), 347-374

{{Short description|Type of Japanese temple}} [[Image:Kumano Jinja 12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama]]]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū]], Shingū, and Nachi [{{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}}].<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> There are more than 3,000 Kumano shrines in Japan, and each has received its [[kami]] from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}.
The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], which comprises {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ([[Shingū, Wakayama]]), [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] ([[Tanabe, Wakayama]]) and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ([[Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture).<ref name="ss">{{Cite web |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |title=Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan" |access-date=2008-06-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090303221919/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |archive-date=2009-03-03 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 and 40 km from each other.<ref name="ss"/> They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Kodō]]|熊野古道}}.<ref name="ss"/> The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]].<ref name="ks"/>
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan.<ref name="ks">{{Cite web |url=http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |title=Kumano Sanzan |access-date=2014-10-15 |archive-date=2019-08-22 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190822161901/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |url-status=dead }}</ref> The area is still considered a place of physical healing.<ref name="ks"/> Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship. In the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with Buddhas. Kuniyasutamahime became associated with ''Sahasrabhūja'' [[Avalokiteśvara]] (''Senju Kannon'', "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (''Yakushi Nyōrai'') and [[Amitābha]] (''Amida Nyōrai'').<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|authorlink1=John Breen (scholar)|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|authorlink2=Mark Teeuwen|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZmMAQAAQBAJ|year=2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|page=194}}</ref>
The site became a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]], the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese [[ethnic religion|indigenous religion]].<ref name="ks"/> The Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century, pilgrims were mostly members of the [[emperor of Japan|imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> The visit was referred to as the {{nihongo|"Kumano ant pilgrimage"|蟻の熊野参り||Ari no Kumano mairi}}, because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.
== See also == *[[Kumano Shrine (Yamagata)]] *''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'' *''[[Acts of Worship]]''
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * D. Max Moerman, ''Localizing Paradise: Kumano Pilgrimage and the Religious Landscape of Premodern Japan''. Harvard University Press, 2004. {{ISBN|0674013956}} * Moerman, David (1997). [https://web.archive.org/web/20140928090538/http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2638 The ideology of landscape and the theater of state: Insei pilgrimage to Kumano (1090–1220)], Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 24 (3-4), 347-374
==External links== {{commonscat|Kumano shrines}} *Japanese Wikipedia article "[[:ja:熊野神社]]" accessed on June 12, 2008 *[http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110925191301/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 |date=2011-09-25 }}
{{Authority control}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Kumano shrines| ]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]

In the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with Buddhas. Kuniyasutamahime became associated with ''Sahasrabhūja'' [[Avalokiteśvara]] (''Senju Kannon'', "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (''Yakushi Nyōrai'') and [[Amitābha]] (''Amida Nyōrai'').<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|authorlink1=John Breen (scholar)|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|authorlink2=Mark Teeuwen|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZmMAQAAQBAJ|year=2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|page=194}}</ref>

{{Short description|Type of Japanese temple}} [[Image:Kumano Jinja 12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama]]]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū]], Shingū, and Nachi [{{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}}].<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> There are more than 3,000 Kumano shrines in [[Japan]], and each has received its [[kami]] from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}.
The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], which comprises {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ([[Shingū, Wakayama]]), [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] ([[Tanabe, Wakayama]]) and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ([[Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture).<ref name="ss">{{Cite web |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |title=Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan" |access-date=2008-06-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090303221919/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |archive-date=2009-03-03 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 and 40 km from each other.<ref name="ss"/> They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Kodō]]|熊野古道}}.<ref name="ss"/> The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]].<ref name="ks"/>
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan.<ref name="ks">{{Cite web |url=http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |title=Kumano Sanzan |access-date=2014-10-15 |archive-date=2019-08-22 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190822161901/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |url-status=dead }}</ref> The area is still considered a place of physical healing.<ref name="ks"/> Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship. In the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with Buddhas. Kuniyasutamahime became associated with ''Sahasrabhūja'' [[Avalokiteśvara]] (''Senju Kannon'', "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (''Yakushi Nyōrai'') and [[Amitābha]] (''Amida Nyōrai'').<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|authorlink1=John Breen (scholar)|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|authorlink2=Mark Teeuwen|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZmMAQAAQBAJ|year=2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|page=194}}</ref>
The site became a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]], the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese [[ethnic religion|indigenous religion]].<ref name="ks"/> The Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century, pilgrims were mostly members of the [[emperor of Japan|imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> The visit was referred to as the {{nihongo|"Kumano ant pilgrimage"|蟻の熊野参り||Ari no Kumano mairi}}, because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.

In the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with Buddhas. Kuniyasutamahime became associated with ''Sahasrabhūja'' [[Avalokiteśvara]] (''Senju Kannon'', "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (''Yakushi Nyōrai'') and [[Amitābha]] (''Amida Nyōrai'').<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|authorlink1=John Breen (scholar)|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|authorlink2=Mark Teeuwen|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZmMAQAAQBAJ|year=2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|page=194}}</ref>
The site became a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]], the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese [[ethnic religion|indigenous religion]].<ref name="ks"/> The Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century, pilgrims were mostly members of the [[emperor of Japan|imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> The visit was referred to as the {{nihongo|"Kumano ant pilgrimage"|蟻の熊野参り||Ari no Kumano mairi}}, because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.

{{Short description|Type of Japanese temple}} [[Image:Kumano Jinja 12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama]]]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū]], Shingū, and Nachi [{{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}}].<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> There are more than 3,000 Kumano shrines in [[Japan]], and each has received its [[kami]] from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}.
The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], which comprises {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ([[Shingū, Wakayama]]), [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] ([[Tanabe, Wakayama]]) and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ([[Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture).<ref name="ss">{{Cite web |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |title=Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan" |access-date=2008-06-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090303221919/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |archive-date=2009-03-03 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 and 40 km from each other.<ref name="ss"/> They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Kodō]]|熊野古道}}.<ref name="ss"/> The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]].<ref name="ks"/>
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan.<ref name="ks">{{Cite web |url=http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |title=Kumano Sanzan |access-date=2014-10-15 |archive-date=2019-08-22 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190822161901/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |url-status=dead }}</ref> The area is still considered a place of physical healing.<ref name="ks"/> Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship. In the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with Buddhas. Kuniyasutamahime became associated with ''Sahasrabhūja'' [[Avalokiteśvara]] (''Senju Kannon'', "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (''Yakushi Nyōrai'') and [[Amitābha]] (''Amida Nyōrai'').<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|authorlink1=John Breen (scholar)|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|authorlink2=Mark Teeuwen|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZmMAQAAQBAJ|year=2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|page=194}}</ref>
The site became a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]], the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese [[ethnic religion|indigenous religion]].<ref name="ks"/> The Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century, pilgrims were mostly members of the [[emperor of Japan|imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> The visit was referred to as the {{nihongo|"Kumano ant pilgrimage"|蟻の熊野参り||Ari no Kumano mairi}}, because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.

{{Short description|Type of Japanese temple}} [[Image:Kumano Jinja 12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama]]]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū]], Shingū, and Nachi [{{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}}].<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> There are more than 3,000 Kumano shrines in [[Japan]], and each has received its [[kami]] from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}.
The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], which comprises {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ([[Shingū, Wakayama]]), [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] ([[Tanabe, Wakayama]]) and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ([[Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture).<ref name="ss">{{Cite web |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |title=Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan" |access-date=2008-06-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090303221919/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |archive-date=2009-03-03 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 and 40 km from each other.<ref name="ss"/> They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Kodō]]|熊野古道}}.<ref name="ss"/> The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]].<ref name="ks"/>
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan.<ref name="ks">{{Cite web |url=http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |title=Kumano Sanzan |access-date=2014-10-15 |archive-date=2019-08-22 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190822161901/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |url-status=dead }}</ref> The area is still considered a place of physical healing.<ref name="ks"/> Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship. In the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with Buddhas. Kuniyasutamahime became associated with ''Sahasrabhūja'' [[Avalokiteśvara]] (''Senju Kannon'', "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (''Yakushi Nyōrai'') and [[Amitābha]] (''Amida Nyōrai'').<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|authorlink1=John Breen (scholar)|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|authorlink2=Mark Teeuwen|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZmMAQAAQBAJ|year=2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|page=194}}</ref>
The site became a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]], the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese [[ethnic religion|indigenous religion]].<ref name="ks"/> The Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century, pilgrims were mostly members of the [[emperor of Japan|imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> The visit was referred to as the {{nihongo|"Kumano ant pilgrimage"|蟻の熊野参り||Ari no Kumano mairi}}, because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.

{{Short description|Type of Japanese temple}} [[Image:Kumano Jinja 12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama]]]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū]], Shingū, and Nachi [{{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}}].<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> There are more than 3,000 Kumano shrines in [[Japan]], and each has received its [[kami]] from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}.
The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], which comprises {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ([[Shingū, Wakayama]]), [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] ([[Tanabe, Wakayama]]) and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ([[Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture).<ref name="ss">{{Cite web |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |title=Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan" |access-date=2008-06-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090303221919/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |archive-date=2009-03-03 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 and 40 km from each other.<ref name="ss"/> They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Kodō]]|熊野古道}}.<ref name="ss"/> The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]].<ref name="ks"/>
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan.<ref name="ks">{{Cite web |url=http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |title=Kumano Sanzan |access-date=2014-10-15 |archive-date=2019-08-22 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190822161901/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |url-status=dead }}</ref> The area is still considered a place of physical healing.<ref name="ks"/> Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship. In the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with Buddhas. Kuniyasutamahime became associated with ''Sahasrabhūja'' [[Avalokiteśvara]] (''Senju Kannon'', "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (''Yakushi Nyōrai'') and [[Amitābha]] (''Amida Nyōrai'').<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|authorlink1=John Breen (scholar)|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|authorlink2=Mark Teeuwen|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZmMAQAAQBAJ|year=2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|page=194}}</ref>
The site became a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]], the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese [[ethnic religion|indigenous religion]].<ref name="ks"/> The Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century, pilgrims were mostly members of the [[emperor of Japan|imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> The visit was referred to as the {{nihongo|"Kumano ant pilgrimage"|蟻の熊野参り||Ari no Kumano mairi}}, because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.

{{Short description|Type of Japanese temple}} [[Image:Kumano Jinja 12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama]]]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū]], Shingū, and Nachi [{{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}}].<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> There are more than 3,000 Kumano shrines in [[Japan]], and each has received its [[kami]] from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}.
The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], which comprises {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ([[Shingū, Wakayama]]), [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] ([[Tanabe, Wakayama]]) and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ([[Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture).<ref name="ss">{{Cite web |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |title=Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan" |access-date=2008-06-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090303221919/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |archive-date=2009-03-03 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 and 40 km from each other.<ref name="ss"/> They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Kodō]]|熊野古道}}.<ref name="ss"/> The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]].<ref name="ks"/>
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan.<ref name="ks">{{Cite web |url=http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |title=Kumano Sanzan |access-date=2014-10-15 |archive-date=2019-08-22 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190822161901/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |url-status=dead }}</ref> The area is still considered a place of physical healing.<ref name="ks"/> Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship. In the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with Buddhas. Kuniyasutamahime became associated with ''Sahasrabhūja'' [[Avalokiteśvara]] (''Senju Kannon'', "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (''Yakushi Nyōrai'') and [[Amitābha]] (''Amida Nyōrai'').<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|authorlink1=John Breen (scholar)|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|authorlink2=Mark Teeuwen|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZmMAQAAQBAJ|year=2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|page=194}}</ref>
The site became a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]], the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese [[ethnic religion|indigenous religion]].<ref name="ks"/> The Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century, pilgrims were mostly members of the [[emperor of Japan|imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> The visit was referred to as the {{nihongo|"Kumano ant pilgrimage"|蟻の熊野参り||Ari no Kumano mairi}}, because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.

{{Short description|Type of Japanese temple}} [[Image:Kumano Jinja 12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama]]]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū]], Shingū, and Nachi [{{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}}].<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> There are more than 3,000 Kumano shrines in [[Japan]], and each has received its [[kami]] from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}.
The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], which comprises {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ([[Shingū, Wakayama]]), [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] ([[Tanabe, Wakayama]]) and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ([[Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture).<ref name="ss">{{Cite web |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |title=Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan" |access-date=2008-06-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090303221919/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |archive-date=2009-03-03 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 and 40 km from each other.<ref name="ss"/> They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Kodō]]|熊野古道}}.<ref name="ss"/> The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]].<ref name="ks"/>
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan.<ref name="ks">{{Cite web |url=http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |title=Kumano Sanzan |access-date=2014-10-15 |archive-date=2019-08-22 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190822161901/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |url-status=dead }}</ref> The area is still considered a place of physical healing.<ref name="ks"/> Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship. In the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with Buddhas. Kuniyasutamahime became associated with ''Sahasrabhūja'' [[Avalokiteśvara]] (''Senju Kannon'', "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (''Yakushi Nyōrai'') and [[Amitābha]] (''Amida Nyōrai'').<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|authorlink1=John Breen (scholar)|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|authorlink2=Mark Teeuwen|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZmMAQAAQBAJ|year=2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|page=194}}</ref>
The site became a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]], the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese [[ethnic religion|indigenous religion]].<ref name="ks"/> The Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century, pilgrims were mostly members of the [[emperor of Japan|imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> The visit was referred to as the {{nihongo|"Kumano ant pilgrimage"|蟻の熊野参り||Ari no Kumano mairi}}, because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.

The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan.<ref name="ks">{{Cite web |url=http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |title=Kumano Sanzan |access-date=2014-10-15 |archive-date=2019-08-22 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190822161901/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |url-status=dead }}</ref> The area is still considered a place of physical healing.<ref name="ks"/> Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship. In the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with Buddhas. Kuniyasutamahime became associated with ''Sahasrabhūja'' [[Avalokiteśvara]] (''Senju Kannon'', "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (''Yakushi Nyōrai'') and [[Amitābha]] (''Amida Nyōrai'').<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|authorlink1=John Breen (scholar)|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|authorlink2=Mark Teeuwen|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZmMAQAAQBAJ|year=2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|page=194}}</ref>
The site became a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]], the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese [[ethnic religion|indigenous religion]].<ref name="ks"/> The Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century, pilgrims were mostly members of the [[emperor of Japan|imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> The visit was referred to as the {{nihongo|"Kumano ant pilgrimage"|蟻の熊野参り||Ari no Kumano mairi}}, because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:NAMESPACE}}-qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|Type of Japanese temple}} [[Image:Kumano Jinja 12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama]]]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū]], Shingū, and Nachi [{{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}}].<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> There are more than 3,000 Kumano shrines in [[Japan]], and each has received its [[kami]] from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}.
The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], which comprises {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ([[Shingū, Wakayama]]), [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] ([[Tanabe, Wakayama]]) and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ([[Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture).<ref name="ss">{{Cite web |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |title=Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan" |access-date=2008-06-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090303221919/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |archive-date=2009-03-03 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 and 40 km from each other.<ref name="ss"/> They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Kodō]]|熊野古道}}.<ref name="ss"/> The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]].<ref name="ks"/>
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan.<ref name="ks">{{Cite web |url=http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |title=Kumano Sanzan |access-date=2014-10-15 |archive-date=2019-08-22 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190822161901/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |url-status=dead }}</ref> The area is still considered a place of physical healing.<ref name="ks"/> Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship. In the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with Buddhas. Kuniyasutamahime became associated with ''Sahasrabhūja'' [[Avalokiteśvara]] (''Senju Kannon'', "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (''Yakushi Nyōrai'') and [[Amitābha]] (''Amida Nyōrai'').<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|authorlink1=John Breen (scholar)|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|authorlink2=Mark Teeuwen|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZmMAQAAQBAJ|year=2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|page=194}}</ref>
The site became a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]], the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese [[ethnic religion|indigenous religion]].<ref name="ks"/> The Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century, pilgrims were mostly members of the [[emperor of Japan|imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> The visit was referred to as the {{nihongo|"Kumano ant pilgrimage"|蟻の熊野参り||Ari no Kumano mairi}}, because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.
== See also == *[[Kumano Shrine (Yamagata)]] *''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'' *''[[Acts of Worship]]''
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * D. Max Moerman, ''Localizing Paradise: Kumano Pilgrimage and the Religious Landscape of Premodern Japan''. Harvard University Press, 2004. {{ISBN|0674013956}} * Moerman, David (1997). [https://web.archive.org/web/20140928090538/http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2638 The ideology of landscape and the theater of state: Insei pilgrimage to Kumano (1090–1220)], Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 24 (3-4), 347-374
==External links== {{commonscat|Kumano shrines}} *Japanese Wikipedia article "[[:ja:熊野神社]]" accessed on June 12, 2008 *[http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110925191301/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 |date=2011-09-25 }}

{{Short description|Type of Japanese temple}} [[Image:Kumano Jinja 12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama]]]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū]], Shingū, and Nachi [{{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}}].<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> There are more than 3,000 Kumano shrines in [[Japan]], and each has received its [[kami]] from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}.
The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], which comprises {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ([[Shingū, Wakayama]]), [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] ([[Tanabe, Wakayama]]) and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ([[Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture).<ref name="ss">{{Cite web |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |title=Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan" |access-date=2008-06-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090303221919/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |archive-date=2009-03-03 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 and 40 km from each other.<ref name="ss"/> They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Kodō]]|熊野古道}}.<ref name="ss"/> The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]].<ref name="ks"/>
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan.<ref name="ks">{{Cite web |url=http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |title=Kumano Sanzan |access-date=2014-10-15 |archive-date=2019-08-22 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190822161901/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |url-status=dead }}</ref> The area is still considered a place of physical healing.<ref name="ks"/> Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship. In the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of [[buddhahood|buddhas]] (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with Buddhas. Kuniyasutamahime became associated with ''Sahasrabhūja'' [[Avalokiteśvara]] (''Senju Kannon'', "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (''Yakushi Nyōrai'') and [[Amitābha]] (''Amida Nyōrai'').<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|authorlink1=John Breen (scholar)|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|authorlink2=Mark Teeuwen|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZmMAQAAQBAJ|year=2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|page=194}}</ref>
The site became a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]], the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese [[ethnic religion|indigenous religion]].<ref name="ks"/> The Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century, pilgrims were mostly members of the [[emperor of Japan|imperial family]] or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> The visit was referred to as the {{nihongo|"Kumano ant pilgrimage"|蟻の熊野参り||Ari no Kumano mairi}}, because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.
