<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kumano shrine]][[es:Santuario kumano]][[fr:Kumano-jinja]][[it:Santuario kumano]][[ja:熊野神社]][[simple:Kumano shrine]][[tr:Kumano tapınağı]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kumano shrine]][[es:Santuario kumano]][[fr:Kumano-jinja]][[it:Santuario kumano]][[ja:熊野神社]][[simple:Kumano shrine]][[tr:Kumano tapınağı]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kumano shrine]][[es:Santuario kumano]][[fr:Kumano-jinja]][[it:Santuario kumano]][[ja:熊野神社]][[simple:Kumano shrine]][[tr:Kumano tapınağı]]
{{Short description|Type of Japanese temple}} [[Image:Kumano Jinja 12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in {{ill|Kurashiki|en|Kurashiki|WD=Q467487|lt=Kurashiki|1=Kurashiki}}, {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}}]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū]], Shingū, and Nachi [{{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}}].<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> There are more than 3,000 Kumano shrines in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, and each has received its [[kami]] from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}.
The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, which comprises {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ({{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|WD=Q823526|lt=Shingū, Wakayama|1=Shingū, Wakayama}}), [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] ({{ill|Tanabe, Wakayama|en|Tanabe, Wakayama|WD=Q304266|lt=Tanabe, Wakayama|1=Tanabe, Wakayama}}) and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ({{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura|1=Nachikatsuura}}, Wakayama Prefecture).<ref name="ss">{{Cite web |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |title=Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan" |access-date=2008-06-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090303221919/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |archive-date=2009-03-03 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 and 40 km from each other.<ref name="ss"/> They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Kodō]]|熊野古道}}.<ref name="ss"/> The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]].<ref name="ks"/>
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan.<ref name="ks">{{Cite web |url=http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |title=Kumano Sanzan |access-date=2014-10-15 |archive-date=2019-08-22 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190822161901/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |url-status=dead }}</ref> The area is still considered a place of physical healing.<ref name="ks"/> Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship. In the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of {{ill|Buddhahood|en|Buddhahood|WD=Q56247062|lt=buddhas|1=Buddhahood}} (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with Buddhas. Kuniyasutamahime became associated with ''Sahasrabhūja'' {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|en|Avalokiteśvara|WD=Q193849|lt=Avalokiteśvara|1=Avalokiteśvara}} (''Senju Kannon'', "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|WD=Q854773|lt=Bhaisajyaguru|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} (''Yakushi Nyōrai'') and [[Amitābha]] (''Amida Nyōrai'').<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|authorlink1=John Breen (scholar)|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|authorlink2=Mark Teeuwen|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZmMAQAAQBAJ|year=2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|page=194}}</ref>
The site became a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]], the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese {{ill|Ethnic religion|en|Ethnic religion|WD=Q1189816|lt=indigenous religion|1=Ethnic religion}}.<ref name="ks"/> The Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century, pilgrims were mostly members of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=imperial family|1=Emperor of Japan}} or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> The visit was referred to as the {{nihongo|"Kumano ant pilgrimage"|蟻の熊野参り||Ari no Kumano mairi}}, because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.
== See also == *[[Kumano Shrine (Yamagata)]] *''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|en|The Tale of the Heike|WD=Q830740|lt=The Tale of the Heike|1=The Tale of the Heike}}'' *''[[Acts of Worship]]''
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * D. Max Moerman, ''Localizing Paradise: Kumano Pilgrimage and the Religious Landscape of Premodern Japan''. Harvard University Press, 2004. {{ISBN|0674013956}} * Moerman, David (1997). [https://web.archive.org/web/20140928090538/http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2638 The ideology of landscape and the theater of state: Insei pilgrimage to Kumano (1090–1220)], Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 24 (3-4), 347-374
==External links== {{commonscat|Kumano shrines}} *Japanese Wikipedia article "[[:ja:熊野神社]]" accessed on June 12, 2008 *[http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110925191301/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 |date=2011-09-25 }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kumano shrine]][[es:Santuario kumano]][[fr:Kumano-jinja]][[it:Santuario kumano]][[ja:熊野神社]][[simple:Kumano shrine]][[tr:Kumano tapınağı]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kumano shrine]][[es:Santuario kumano]][[fr:Kumano-jinja]][[it:Santuario kumano]][[ja:熊野神社]][[simple:Kumano shrine]][[tr:Kumano tapınağı]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kumano shrine]][[es:Santuario kumano]][[fr:Kumano-jinja]][[it:Santuario kumano]][[ja:熊野神社]][[simple:Kumano shrine]][[tr:Kumano tapınağı]]
{{Short description|Type of Japanese temple}} [[Image:Kumano Jinja 12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in {{ill|Kurashiki|en|Kurashiki|qid=Q467487|lt=Kurashiki|1=Kurashiki}}, {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|qid=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}}]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū]], Shingū, and Nachi [{{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}}].<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> There are more than 3,000 Kumano shrines in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, and each has received its [[kami]] from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}.
The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|qid=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, which comprises {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ({{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|qid=Q823526|lt=Shingū, Wakayama|1=Shingū, Wakayama}}), [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] ({{ill|Tanabe, Wakayama|en|Tanabe, Wakayama|qid=Q304266|lt=Tanabe, Wakayama|1=Tanabe, Wakayama}}) and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ({{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|qid=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura|1=Nachikatsuura}}, Wakayama Prefecture).<ref name="ss">{{Cite web |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |title=Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan" |access-date=2008-06-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090303221919/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |archive-date=2009-03-03 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 and 40 km from each other.<ref name="ss"/> They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Kodō]]|熊野古道}}.<ref name="ss"/> The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]].<ref name="ks"/>
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan.<ref name="ks">{{Cite web |url=http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |title=Kumano Sanzan |access-date=2014-10-15 |archive-date=2019-08-22 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190822161901/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |url-status=dead }}</ref> The area is still considered a place of physical healing.<ref name="ks"/> Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship. In the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of {{ill|Buddhahood|en|Buddhahood|qid=Q56247062|lt=buddhas|1=Buddhahood}} (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with Buddhas. Kuniyasutamahime became associated with ''Sahasrabhūja'' {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|en|Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q193849|lt=Avalokiteśvara|1=Avalokiteśvara}} (''Senju Kannon'', "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Bhaisajyaguru|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} (''Yakushi Nyōrai'') and [[Amitābha]] (''Amida Nyōrai'').<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|authorlink1=John Breen (scholar)|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|authorlink2=Mark Teeuwen|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZmMAQAAQBAJ|year=2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|page=194}}</ref>
The site became a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]], the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese {{ill|Ethnic religion|en|Ethnic religion|qid=Q1189816|lt=indigenous religion|1=Ethnic religion}}.<ref name="ks"/> The Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century, pilgrims were mostly members of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=imperial family|1=Emperor of Japan}} or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> The visit was referred to as the {{nihongo|"Kumano ant pilgrimage"|蟻の熊野参り||Ari no Kumano mairi}}, because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.
== See also == *[[Kumano Shrine (Yamagata)]] *''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|en|The Tale of the Heike|qid=Q830740|lt=The Tale of the Heike|1=The Tale of the Heike}}'' *''[[Acts of Worship]]''
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * D. Max Moerman, ''Localizing Paradise: Kumano Pilgrimage and the Religious Landscape of Premodern Japan''. Harvard University Press, 2004. {{ISBN|0674013956}} * Moerman, David (1997). [https://web.archive.org/web/20140928090538/http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2638 The ideology of landscape and the theater of state: Insei pilgrimage to Kumano (1090–1220)], Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 24 (3-4), 347-374
==External links== {{commonscat|Kumano shrines}} *Japanese Wikipedia article "[[:ja:熊野神社]]" accessed on June 12, 2008 *[http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110925191301/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 |date=2011-09-25 }}

{{Short description|Type of Japanese temple}} [[Image:Kumano Jinja 12.jpg|275 px|thumb|A Kumano shrine in {{ill|Kurashiki|en|Kurashiki|qid=Q467487|lt=Kurashiki|1=Kurashiki}}, {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|qid=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}}]]A '''{{nihongo|Kumano shrine|熊野神社|Kumano Jinja}}''' is a type of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} which enshrines the three Kumano mountains: [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū]], Shingū, and Nachi [{{nihongo|Kumano [[Gongen]]|熊野権現}}].<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> There are more than 3,000 Kumano shrines in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, and each has received its [[kami]] from another Kumano shrine through a process of propagation called {{nihongo|''[[bunrei]]''|分霊}} or {{nihongo|''[[kanjō]]''|勧請}}.
The point of origin of the Kumano cult is the Kumano Sanzan shrine complex in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|qid=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, which comprises {{nihongo|[[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]]|熊野速玉大社}} ({{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|qid=Q823526|lt=Shingū, Wakayama|1=Shingū, Wakayama}}), [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] ({{ill|Tanabe, Wakayama|en|Tanabe, Wakayama|qid=Q304266|lt=Tanabe, Wakayama|1=Tanabe, Wakayama}}) and [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] ({{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|qid=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura|1=Nachikatsuura}}, Wakayama Prefecture).<ref name="ss">{{Cite web |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |title=Sacred site "Kumano Sanzan" |access-date=2008-06-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090303221919/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_e.html |archive-date=2009-03-03 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
==Kumano Sanzan == [[Image:Waterfall Nachi02.jpg|thumb|200 px|Nachi Falls and Seiganto-ji]]The three Kumano Sanzan shrines are the ''Sōhonsha'' ("head shrines") of all Kumano shrines and lie between 20 and 40 km from each other.<ref name="ss"/> They are connected to each other by the pilgrimage route known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Kodō]]|熊野古道}}.<ref name="ss"/> The great Kumano Sanzan complex also includes two Buddhist temples, [[Seiganto-ji]] and [[Fudarakusan-ji]].<ref name="ks"/>
The religious significance of the Kumano region goes back to prehistoric times and therefore predates all modern religions in Japan.<ref name="ks">{{Cite web |url=http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |title=Kumano Sanzan |access-date=2014-10-15 |archive-date=2019-08-22 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190822161901/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano_sanzan/ |url-status=dead }}</ref> The area is still considered a place of physical healing.<ref name="ks"/> Each shrine initially had its own separate form of nature worship. In the 10th century, under the influence of Buddhism, the three came to be worshiped together as the three deities of Kumano.<ref name="ks"/> Because at the time Japanese ''kami'' were believed to be emanations of {{ill|Buddhahood|en|Buddhahood|qid=Q56247062|lt=buddhas|1=Buddhahood}} (''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory), the three came to be associated with Buddhas. Kuniyasutamahime became associated with ''Sahasrabhūja'' {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|en|Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q193849|lt=Avalokiteśvara|1=Avalokiteśvara}} (''Senju Kannon'', "[[Avalokiteśvara#Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara|Thousand-Armed Avalokiteśvara]]"), {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Bhaisajyaguru|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} (''Yakushi Nyōrai'') and [[Amitābha]] (''Amida Nyōrai'').<ref name="ss"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|authorlink1=John Breen (scholar)|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|authorlink2=Mark Teeuwen|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZmMAQAAQBAJ|year=2013|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|page=194}}</ref>
The site became a unique example of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]], the fusion between Buddhism and Japanese {{ill|Ethnic religion|en|Ethnic religion|qid=Q1189816|lt=indigenous religion|1=Ethnic religion}}.<ref name="ks"/> The Kumano Sanzan site attracted many worshipers and became a popular pilgrimage destination.<ref name="ss"/> In the 11th century, pilgrims were mostly members of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=imperial family|1=Emperor of Japan}} or aristocrats, but four centuries later they were mostly commoners.<ref name="ks"/> The visit was referred to as the {{nihongo|"Kumano ant pilgrimage"|蟻の熊野参り||Ari no Kumano mairi}}, because they could be seen winding through the valleys like so many ants.
